background: Refer to Example 5.4 to calculate the standard deviations of the sampling distributions of the sample mean $\bar{x}$ and the sample median $m$. Additionally, determine which statistic appears to be a better estimator of the population mean $\mu$.

task.query: Find the variance and standard deviation of the sampling distribution of $\bar{x}$.
task.answer:
The variance of the sampling distribution of $\bar{x}$ is calculated as:
$$
\sigma_{\bar{x}}^2=E\left[[\bar{x}-E(\bar{x})]^2\right\}=\Sigma(\bar{x}-\mu)^2 p(\bar{x})
$$
where, from Example 5.4,
$$
E(\bar{x})=\mu=.556
$$
Then,
$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_X^2 & =(-5-.556)^2\left(\frac{1}{729}\right)+(-3.33-.556)^2\left(\frac{18}{729}\right)+\cdots(5-.556)^2\left(\frac{8}{729}\right) \\
& =2.675
\end{aligned}
$$
and
$$
\sigma_{\bar{x}}=\sqrt{2.675}=1.64
$$

task.query: Find the variance and standard deviation of the sampling distribution of $m$.
task.answer:
The variance of the sampling distribution of $m$ is calculated as:
$$
\sigma_m^2=E\left\{[m-E(m)]^2\right\}
$$
where, from Example 5.4, the expected value of $m$ is $E(m)=.460$. Then,
$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_m^2 & =E\left\{[m-E(m)]^2\right\}=\Sigma[m-E(m)]^2 p(m) \\
& =(-5-.460)\left(\frac{25}{729}\right)+(0-.460)\left(\frac{612}{729}\right)+(5-.460)\left(\frac{92}{729}\right)=3.801
\end{aligned}
$$
and
$$
\sigma_m=\sqrt{3.801}=1.95
$$

task.query: Which statistic appears to be the better estimator for the population mean $\mu$, the sample mean $\bar{x}$ or the sample median $m$?
task.answer: The sampling distribution of the sample median $m$ is biased (i.e., it is located to the left of the mean $\mu$), and its standard deviation $\sigma_m=1.95$ is larger than the standard deviation of the sampling distribution of $\bar{x}, \sigma_{\bar{x}}=1.64$. Consequently, for the population in question, the sample mean $\bar{x}$ would be a better estimator of the population mean $\mu$ than the sample median $m$ would be.

meta_info.caption: Variance of Estimators -Craps Application
meta_info.section: 5.2 Properties of Sampling Distributions: Unbiasedness and Minimum Variance
